title: On a Conjecture of Veech About Möbius Orthogonality
speaker: Thierry de la Rue
abstract: In unpublished lecture notes, William A. Veech considered the following potential property of the Möbius function: "In any Furstenberg system of the Möbius function, the zero-coordinate is orthogonal to any function measurable with respect to the Pinsker sigma-algebra of the system." Veech proved that this property would imply the validity of Sarnak's conjecture, that is orthogonality of the Möbius function to any topological dynamical system of zero entropy. He also conjectured that both properties are equivalent.
tags: Workshop on Dynamics, Discrete Analysis and Multiplicative Number Theory

proof_stages.label: Sequences, dynamical systems, visible measures, and Furstenberg systems
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up bounded sequences, topological dynamical systems, measure-preserving systems, empirical visible measures from orbits, and Furstenberg systems obtained by viewing a sequence as a point in the shift space.

proof_stages.label: Sarnak, Chowla, and Veech's Pinsker formulation
proof_stages.summary: Sarnak orthogonality is recalled, Chowla's higher-correlation condition is presented as a stronger intrinsic condition, and Veech's conjectural condition is formulated as vanishing of the zero-coordinate conditional expectation on the Pinsker factor of every Furstenberg system.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic classes and maximal factors
proof_stages.summary: The zero-entropy/Pinsker setup is abstracted to characteristic classes of measure-preserving systems, stable under factors and countable joinings, for which every system has a largest F-factor A_F.

proof_stages.label: Joining control through the maximal F-factor
proof_stages.summary: For any joining of an arbitrary system with an F-system, correlations with functions from the F-system must pass through the largest F-factor, so functions orthogonal to A_F have zero such correlations.

proof_stages.label: Generalized Veech property and the topological class C_F
proof_stages.summary: For a characteristic class F, the Veech property of a sequence is defined by vanishing of the zero-coordinate conditional expectation onto A_F in every Furstenberg system, and C_F is defined as the class of topological systems whose visible measures yield F-systems.

proof_stages.label: Proof that the Veech property implies orthogonality to C_F
proof_stages.summary: Given a C_F topological system, product empirical measures along subsequences converge to joinings of a Furstenberg system of u with a visible F-measure, and the joining-control proposition plus the Veech property forces every correlation limit to be zero.

proof_stages.label: Converse theorem for EC characteristic classes
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the ergodic-component closure F^c and EC classes satisfying F=F^c, then states that for EC classes the converse holds: orthogonality to all systems in C_F implies the generalized Veech property.

proof_stages.label: Finite-alphabet combinatorial interpretation
proof_stages.summary: In the finite-valued zero-entropy case, the Pinsker sigma-algebra of a Furstenberg system is identified with a tail sigma-algebra, yielding a concrete tail-correlation criterion and, for {-1,1}-valued sequences, a Chowla-like large-gap condition.

examples.name: Möbius/Liouville as bounded sequences
examples.note: The Möbius or Liouville function is offered as the motivating number-theoretic instance of the abstract bounded sequence u, while multiplicativity is explicitly not used in the general argument.
examples.engagement_reason: Möbius/Liouville are invoked as motivating bounded sequences for Sarnak/Chowla/Veech, but no concrete computation or verification is performed on them.

examples.name: Identity systems as a characteristic class
examples.note: Systems whose transformation is the identity illustrate the closure definition of a characteristic class and make the maximal factor concrete as the invariant sigma-algebra.
examples.engagement_reason: The identity class is used to apply the maximal-factor construction, with the largest identity factor identified as the invariant sigma-algebra.

examples.name: Discrete-spectrum systems
examples.note: The discrete-spectrum class illustrates characteristic classes via the Kronecker factor and later serves as a comparison case for non-EC behavior and visible versus all invariant measures.
examples.engagement_reason: Discrete-spectrum systems are treated as a characteristic class, their maximal factor is identified as the Kronecker factor, and the class is discussed as non-EC in the Q&A.

examples.note: Inverse limits of nilsystems, with their Host-Kra structure factors, are used as a familiar family showing that the characteristic-class formalism covers standard ergodic-theoretic structure theory.
examples.engagement_reason: The nilsystem inverse-limit class is used in the characteristic-class framework, with its maximal factor identified as the Host–Kra structure factor and its EC status discussed.

examples.name: Two-valued sequences and finite-coordinate products
examples.note: Specializing the finite-alphabet interpretation to sequences taking values in {-1,1} and to products of finitely many coordinates shows that Veech's condition resembles a Chowla-type large-gap correlation condition.
examples.engagement_reason: For finite and then two-valued sequences, the speaker derives a tail/Pinsker and finite-coordinate product characterization leading to an asymptotic Chowla-type condition.

passes.reason: The ±1 sequences and finite-coordinate products are ordinary symbolic/cylinder-function test cases for a Chowla-type formulation.

examples.name: Visible measures discrete-spectrum but other invariant measures not
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions topological systems where all visible measures have discrete spectrum although some invariant measures do not, illustrating why C_F is defined using visible measures.
examples.engagement_reason: The existence of examples with all visible measures discrete-spectrum but other invariant measures not is stated in response to a question, without constructing or analyzing such an example.
